statement: Next.js is built around a hybrid rendering philosophy: the same codebase can serve pages via Static Site Generation (SSG, built at compile time), Server-Side Rendering (SSR, rendered per request), Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR, statically served and revalidated in the background after a configurable interval), and Streaming (App Router progressive HTML flushing via React Suspense). The correct choice is determined per route by data freshness requirements and personalization needs, not by a global setting. SSG/ISR is preferred for performance and cost; SSR is reserved for pages that require request-time data (cookies, auth headers); Streaming reduces TTFB for slow data.

The App Router uses a file-system convention where the directory structure under app/ defines routes. Special filenames have fixed semantics: page.tsx renders the route UI, layout.tsx wraps a route segment and its children persistently, loading.tsx is an automatic Suspense boundary shown during navigation, error.tsx is an Error Boundary for that segment, not-found.tsx handles notFound() calls, and route.ts defines HTTP API handlers. Colocation of components, tests, and styles is allowed inside route segments because only files named with these reserved names are exposed as routes. This replaces the pages/ directory convention from the Pages Router.

Adding 'use client' to a component marks it and all its imported subtree as Client Components, opting the entire branch out of server rendering and React Server Components benefits (zero bundle size, direct data access, no hydration cost). The common antipattern is placing 'use client' at a high level in the tree (e.g., a layout or large feature component) because one child needs onClick or useState, when the correct fix is to extract only the interactive leaf into its own Client Component. Overuse of 'use client' defeats the App Router's performance model, bloats the JS bundle sent to the browser, and prevents async Server Components from fetching data at the point of use.

Next.js App Router caches aggressively by default at four layers: the Request Memoization cache (per-render deduplication), the Data Cache (persistent across requests, opt-out with cache:'no-store' or revalidate:0), the Full Route Cache (static HTML stored on disk/CDN), and the Router Cache (client-side segment cache). The failure mode is deploying dynamic data fetches without explicit cache opt-out and seeing stale data served indefinitely. Common symptoms: API responses not updating after writes, POST Server Actions not invalidating GET data, new deployments still serving old content. Fix by calling revalidatePath() / revalidateTag() in Server Actions after mutations, or setting fetch({ next: { revalidate: N } }) appropriately.

Server Actions are POST endpoints automatically created by Next.js for functions marked with 'use server'. Security implications: (1) they are publicly reachable HTTP endpoints — any user can call them directly with a crafted request, so authorization checks (session validation, RBAC) must be inside the action body, not only in the UI that invokes them; (2) Server Action IDs are exposed in the client bundle and can be enumerated; (3) input must be validated server-side (use zod or similar) because Client Components pass arguments over the network; (4) CSRF protection is built in via the Origin header check in Next.js ≥ 14.1, but only when invoked via form — direct fetch calls bypass this.

Next.js provides three request-handling primitives with distinct execution contexts and capabilities. Middleware (middleware.ts at project root) runs on the Edge Runtime before routing on every matched request — it can read/modify request headers and cookies and redirect/rewrite, but cannot access Node.js APIs, databases, or large npm packages. Route Handlers (app/*/route.ts) run in the Node.js runtime (or Edge if opted in) and are full HTTP handlers supporting all methods; they replace API Routes from the Pages Router. API Routes (pages/api/*.ts) are the Pages Router equivalent. Use middleware for auth redirects and A/B testing header injection; use Route Handlers for REST endpoints and webhook receivers; never put heavy computation in middleware.

A Next.js App Router route segment is statically rendered (at build time) unless it opts into dynamic rendering. Dynamic rendering is triggered automatically when the segment uses: cookies(), headers(), or searchParams (dynamic APIs that are request-specific), or a fetch() with cache:'no-store' or revalidate:0. If any component in the segment tree triggers dynamic rendering, the entire segment becomes dynamic. The decision tree: prefer static + ISR (revalidate interval) by default; use Partial Prerendering (PPR, experimental) to statically shell with streaming dynamic islands; fall back to full dynamic only when personalization or real-time data is mandatory.

next/image automatically handles responsive sizing (srcset generation), lazy loading, format conversion to WebP/AVIF, and prevents layout shift by requiring width/height or fill props. The critical configuration points: the 'domains' or 'remotePatterns' allowlist in next.config.js must explicitly permit external image hostnames, and omitting 'priority' on above-the-fold images will defer loading and hurt LCP. next/font (Google Fonts and local fonts) eliminates the external network request at runtime by embedding font CSS at build time and uses font-display:swap by default, preventing render-blocking font loads. Both optimizations require no CDN setup and work in self-hosted deployments.

Parallel Routes (@folder convention) allow rendering multiple independent route segments in the same layout slot simultaneously, each with its own loading and error states. They are used for dashboards with independently loading panels, or conditional UI (e.g., show modal OR full page based on navigation context). Intercepting Routes ((.) and (..) conventions) allow a route to render inside the current layout when navigated to via client-side navigation, but as a standalone page when accessed directly or on refresh — the primary use case is photo gallery modals that show as overlays during browsing but as full pages when shared via URL. These two patterns are often composed together (modal routes).

Deploying Next.js on Vercel vs. self-hosted has significant capability and operational differences. Vercel provides native support for all Next.js features (ISR, Edge Middleware, Image Optimization, Server Actions, Streaming) with zero configuration, automatic CDN revalidation via revalidateTag(), and preview deployments per branch. Self-hosted deployments (Docker with 'output:standalone', or node server.js) support all Node.js runtime features but require manual CDN setup for static asset caching, a separate image optimization service or relying on built-in server-side optimization without a CDN, and manual cache revalidation plumbing. Edge Middleware in self-hosted mode requires an edge-compatible reverse proxy (e.g., Cloudflare Workers). The standalone output mode bundles only the required node_modules for minimal Docker image size.

Next.js App Router Server Components cannot use React hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext) or browser-only APIs (window, document, localStorage) because they execute exclusively on the server. The subtle failure mode is that adding a hook to a server component produces a build-time error, but importing a third-party library that internally uses hooks (e.g., a UI component library) inside a Server Component causes the same error in a hard-to-trace way. The diagnostic is: find the chain of imports from the Server Component to the code calling the hook. Fix either by wrapping the third-party component in a 'use client' leaf wrapper, or by lazy-loading the component with next/dynamic and ssr:false if it is purely decorative and not needed for initial render.

Next.js Partial Prerendering (PPR, available as experimental since Next 14) splits a route into a static shell rendered at build time and dynamic holes that stream in per-request via Suspense boundaries. The mental model: wrap slow-loading personalized content in <Suspense fallback={...}> and the framework serves the outer shell instantly from CDN while the dynamic parts stream. The constraint is that PPR requires all dynamic APIs (cookies(), headers()) to be called inside Suspense boundaries — using them outside causes the entire route to fall back to full dynamic rendering, silently negating the optimization. Enable with `experimental: { ppr: true }` and audit with `next build --debug` output showing route rendering modes.

Next.js middleware running on the Edge Runtime has a 1MB code size limit (compressed) and cannot import Node.js native modules (fs, crypto, net, etc.), use npm packages that depend on Node built-ins, or execute long-running synchronous work. A common production failure is importing a JWT validation library that transitively depends on the Node.js crypto module, causing middleware to fail silently or throw at the edge. The fix is to use the Web Crypto API (available in Edge Runtime via globalThis.crypto) for cryptographic operations, or use jose for JWT handling as it is Edge-compatible. Always test middleware in edge preview mode locally before deploying.

Next.js App Router provides the generateStaticParams function for pre-rendering dynamic routes at build time. The key behavior: for a route like app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx, returning an array of { slug } objects from generateStaticParams() causes Next.js to statically generate one HTML page per combination. Combined with revalidate, this enables ISR for dynamic segments — the page is statically served and regenerated in the background. The performance implication: for sites with thousands of pages, generating all params at build time is slow; set dynamicParams = true (default) and only generate the most critical pages in generateStaticParams, allowing less-visited pages to be rendered on first request and then cached.

Next.js instrumentation.ts (stable since Next 15) allows running code once when the server starts, before any requests are served. This is the correct place for: (1) OpenTelemetry SDK initialization; (2) registering global error trackers (Sentry.init); (3) warming caches or connections at startup. The file must export a register() function at the root of the project (not inside app/ or pages/). Before instrumentation.ts was available, teams used _app.tsx or middleware for this, causing either double-initialization on hot reloads or edge-runtime incompatibility. The register() function runs in both Node.js and Edge runtimes, so guard Node-specific initialization with process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs'.

Next.js generateMetadata function (App Router) enables dynamic SEO metadata including Open Graph, Twitter cards, and canonical URLs. The critical pattern is that generateMetadata is a Server Component async function that runs on the server per-request for dynamic routes, so it can fetch from databases or external APIs without client-side rendering. For static routes, return a plain metadata object instead. A common mistake is duplicating metadata in both the layout and page — layout metadata is merged with page metadata, with page taking precedence on conflict. For i18n sites, use the alternates.languages map to emit hreflang tags, and set metadataBase in the root layout to resolve relative image URLs correctly for social sharing previews.

Calling cookies() or headers() inside a Server Component that is exported with revalidate > 0 or from a generateStaticParams route causes a Next.js build error because static generation cannot depend on request-time values. The failure is confusing because the error message points to the dynamic API call, not the revalidate configuration. The root design issue: you must choose between static (deterministic at build time) and dynamic (per-request) rendering — you cannot have both. The fix is either remove revalidate to make the route fully dynamic, or restructure to move the cookies()/headers() call into a separate Client Component or a different Server Component that is explicitly opt-in to dynamic rendering via `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`.

Testing Next.js App Router applications requires strategy adaptations compared to Pages Router. Unit testing Server Components directly is not yet officially supported by React Testing Library (as of early 2025) because RSC cannot run in jsdom. The recommended strategy is: (1) test Server Component data-fetching logic in isolation by extracting it to pure async functions testable without the component; (2) use Playwright or Cypress for integration tests that exercise actual rendering; (3) use react-testing-library + jest/vitest for Client Components only; (4) for API routes, test with supertest or fetch against a running Next.js server. The msw (Mock Service Worker) library works in both browser and Node.js contexts for mocking fetch calls in tests.

Next.js bundle analysis via @next/bundle-analyzer is the primary tool for diagnosing client-side JavaScript weight. Run ANALYZE=true next build to generate an interactive treemap showing what is in each JS chunk. Common findings that warrant action: (1) date-fns or lodash imported without tree-shaking (use date-fns/format instead of date-fns); (2) large third-party UI libraries imported wholesale; (3) code that should be server-only accidentally included in client bundles. The server-only package (import 'server-only') throws a build-time error if a file is ever imported in a Client Component, making it the correct guard for server-side secrets and large server dependencies that must not ship to browsers.

Next.js environment variable handling has three tiers with strict client exposure rules. Variables without a prefix are only available server-side (process.env.DB_URL — never sent to browser). Variables prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_ are inlined at build time into the client bundle — they are baked into the JavaScript and cannot change without a rebuild, and they are fully visible to anyone who inspects the page source. For runtime server configuration that changes per deployment without a rebuild, use runtime env vars accessed only in server code or via a /api route. The common security failure is putting secrets in NEXT_PUBLIC_ because they happened to also be needed client-side — the correct fix is to expose only a derived non-secret value via a server endpoint.
